comment_text.sn22.1:1.1: For a general survey of the topic of the five aggregates, see my Introduction to the Linked Discourses. The main terms are mostly the same as in dependent origination, for which see my notes to SN 12.1 and SN 12.2. The exception is saññā, which I discuss in the next Sutta (SN 22.2:7.8).
comment_text.sn22.1:1.2: The Bhaggas were an isolated republican clan, sandwiched between the Vacchas south of the Yamuna and the Kosalans north of the Ganges. By the time of the Buddha they were evidently subject to the Vacchas. | “Crocodile’s Bellow” (susumāragira) was their capital. The commentary it was was so-named because a crocodile bellowed in a nearby lake during the founding of the city.
comment_text.sn22.1:1.3: Nakula’s parents were an ideal married couple (AN 1.257, AN 1.266) who wanted to be together in this life and the next (AN 4.55). The father recovered from a previous illness with the help of his wife (AN 6.16). Each features independently in Suttas as well (SN 22.1, SN 35.131, AN 8.48). Nothing is known of their son Nakula himself, nor are their personal names recorded.
comment_text.sn22.1:2.2: Aniccadassavī is literally “not a regular seer” where nicca has the sense, “regular, routine”.
comment_text.sn22.1:3.2: The application of the vivid idiom “swaddled in its shell” is found in AN 8.11:12.1: “in this population lost in ignorance, swaddled in their shells, I alone have broken open the egg of ignorance and realized the supreme perfect awakening.” See too the simile of the chook hatching eggs at SN 22.101:3.1.
comment_text.sn22.1:3.6: This brief teaching exemplifies the Buddha’s method. First he affirms Nakula’s father’s complaint, not dismissing it or trying to make him look at the positive. He is genuinely suffering, he accepts that, and so does the Buddha. Then the Buddha offers encouragement, not false hope, but giving a meaningful reflection. Contrast this with modern gurus who claim physical ailments can be cured by the mind.
comment_text.sn22.1:5.2: Soma, a ritual drink of milky color with psychotropic effects, was described as “immortal” (amṛta) in the Rig Veda (RV 9.84.2), since it was stolen from heaven by a falcon (RV 4.26, 4.27), and since it confers immortality (RV 8.48.3). By the time of the Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa the drug itself was called “ambrosia”. That the soma is alluded to here as the “deathless ambrosia” (amata; Sanskrit amṛta) is shown by the use of the verb abhisitta (“anointed”, literally “sprinkled”). This is typically used for royal consecration, which is fitting as soma is often styled “king”. ŚB 9.4.4.8 describes how the sacrificer, with divine authority, is “anointed with deathless ambrosia” (amṛtābhiṣikto) by drinking King Soma. It is here used idiomatically.
comment_text.sn22.1:9.2: This section is similar to MN 1:3.1. | An “unlearned ordinary person”, who has not realized any of the stages of the noble path, is contrasted with one who has entered the path. | “Noble one” (ariya) loosely conveys the sense “cultured” or “civilized”; it is a term for the inheritors of the Aryan culture that originated among the proto-Indo-European peoples of the central Asian steppes. | “True person” (sappurisa) indicates one who is authentic and genuine in their realization of the truth, and hence is virtuous and good. Both “noble one” and “true person” are technical terms referring to any person who has at least entered the path to stream-entry.
comment_text.sn22.1:9.3: Each of these phrases expresses a particular mode of conceiving the self; I give a few examples. | “Form as self” (ablative): form is the same as self, or seen from the viewpoint of self, or in terms of self; this can include all the others. It might also be “self derived from form”, for example a person who identifies with their mind, which they believe is created by the brain. | “Self as having form” (possessive): the self owns form like a possession, as a soul might possess a body. Or else form is an attribute of self, as a person in the human realm is endowed with a body. | “Form in self” (locative): form is a part of self, as a body is only part of what makes a person. Or else it could be “form in reference to self”, such as a narcissist who believes their body is the epitome of beauty. | “Self in form” (locative): self is a part of form, such as the heart or brain. Or else “self in reference to form”, such as a person who defines their self through race.
comment_text.sn22.1:9.4: Compare Kaivalya Upaniṣad 20: “I am the form of Śiva” (ahaṁ śivarūpam asmi). | Form may be internal or external, so the form that is owned as “mine” may be the body, or else one’s possessions, totems, family, or any other material thing to which one is attached.

root_text.sn22.1:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā bhaggesu viharati susumāragire bhesakaḷāvane migadāye.
root_text.sn22.1:1.3: Atha kho nakulapitā gahapati yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho nakulapitā gahapati bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn22.1:2.1: “Ahamasmi, bhante, jiṇṇo vuḍḍho mahallako addhagato vayoanuppatto āturakāyo abhikkhaṇātaṅko.
root_text.sn22.1:2.2: Aniccadassāvī kho panāhaṁ, bhante, bhagavato manobhāvanīyānañca bhikkhūnaṁ.
root_text.sn22.1:3.2: Āturo hāyaṁ, gahapati, kāyo aṇḍabhūto pariyonaddho.
root_text.sn22.1:3.3: Yo hi, gahapati, imaṁ kāyaṁ pariharanto muhuttampi ārogyaṁ paṭijāneyya, kimaññatra bālyā?
root_text.sn22.1:4.1: Atha kho nakulapitā gahapati bhagavato bhāsitaṁ abhinanditvā anumoditvā uṭṭhāyāsanā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā padakkhiṇaṁ katvā yenāyasmā sāriputto tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā āyasmantaṁ sāriputtaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinnaṁ kho nakulapitaraṁ gahapatiṁ āyasmā sāriputto etadavoca:
root_text.sn22.1:4.4: Alattha no ajja bhagavato sammukhā dhammiṁ kathaṁ savanāyā”ti?
root_text.sn22.1:5.2: Idānāhaṁ, bhante, bhagavatā dhammiyā kathāya amatena abhisitto”ti.
root_text.sn22.1:5.3: “Yathā kathaṁ pana tvaṁ, gahapati, bhagavatā dhammiyā kathāya amatena abhisitto”ti?
root_text.sn22.1:5.4: “Idhāhaṁ, bhante, yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkamiṁ; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdiṁ. Ekamantaṁ nisinno khvāhaṁ, bhante, bhagavantaṁ etadavocaṁ:
root_text.sn22.1:5.5: ‘ahamasmi, bhante, jiṇṇo vuḍḍho mahallako addhagato vayoanuppatto āturakāyo abhikkhaṇātaṅko.
root_text.sn22.1:5.6: Aniccadassāvī kho panāhaṁ, bhante, bhagavato manobhāvanīyānañca bhikkhūnaṁ.
root_text.sn22.1:6.1: Evaṁ vutte, maṁ, bhante, bhagavā etadavoca:
root_text.sn22.1:6.3: Āturo hāyaṁ, gahapati, kāyo aṇḍabhūto pariyonaddho.
root_text.sn22.1:6.4: Yo hi, gahapati, imaṁ kāyaṁ pariharanto muhuttampi ārogyaṁ paṭijāneyya, kimaññatra bālyā?
root_text.sn22.1:6.6: āturakāyassa me sato cittaṁ anāturaṁ bhavissatīti.
root_text.sn22.1:6.8: Evaṁ khvāhaṁ, bhante, bhagavatā dhammiyā kathāya amatena abhisitto”ti.
root_text.sn22.1:7.1: “Na hi pana taṁ, gahapati, paṭibhāsi bhagavantaṁ uttariṁ paṭipucchituṁ:
root_text.sn22.1:7.2: ‘kittāvatā nu kho, bhante, āturakāyo ceva hoti āturacitto ca, kittāvatā ca pana āturakāyo hi kho hoti no ca āturacitto’”ti?
root_text.sn22.1:7.3: “Dūratopi kho mayaṁ, bhante, āgaccheyyāma āyasmato sāriputtassa santike etassa bhāsitassa atthamaññātuṁ.
root_text.sn22.1:7.4: Sādhu vatāyasmantaṁyeva sāriputtaṁ paṭibhātu etassa bhāsitassa attho”ti.
root_text.sn22.1:8.1: “Tena hi, gahapati, suṇāhi, sādhukaṁ manasi karohi; bhāsissāmī”ti.
root_text.sn22.1:8.2: “Evaṁ, bhante”ti kho nakulapitā gahapati āyasmato sāriputtassa paccassosi.
root_text.sn22.1:9.1: “Kathañca, gahapati, āturakāyo ceva hoti, āturacitto ca?
root_text.sn22.1:9.2: Idha, gahapati, assutavā puthujjano ariyānaṁ adassāvī ariyadhammassa akovido ariyadhamme avinīto sappurisānaṁ adassāvī sappurisadhammassa akovido sappurisadhamme avinīto
root_text.sn22.1:9.3: rūpaṁ attato samanupassati, rūpavantaṁ vā attānaṁ; attani vā rūpaṁ, rūpasmiṁ vā attānaṁ.
root_text.sn22.1:9.5: Tassa ‘ahaṁ rūpaṁ, mama rūpan’ti pariyuṭṭhaṭṭhāyino taṁ rūpaṁ vipariṇamati aññathā hoti.
root_text.sn22.1:10.1: Vedanaṁ attato samanupassati, vedanāvantaṁ vā attānaṁ; attani vā vedanaṁ, vedanāya vā attānaṁ.
root_text.sn22.1:10.3: Tassa ‘ahaṁ vedanā, mama vedanā’ti pariyuṭṭhaṭṭhāyino, sā vedanā vipariṇamati aññathā hoti.
root_text.sn22.1:11.1: Saññaṁ attato samanupassati, saññāvantaṁ vā attānaṁ; attani vā saññaṁ, saññāya vā attānaṁ.
root_text.sn22.1:11.3: Tassa ‘ahaṁ saññā, mama saññā’ti pariyuṭṭhaṭṭhāyino, sā saññā vipariṇamati aññathā hoti.
root_text.sn22.1:12.1: Saṅkhāre attato samanupassati, saṅkhāravantaṁ vā attānaṁ; attani vā saṅkhāre, saṅkhāresu vā attānaṁ.
root_text.sn22.1:12.3: Tassa ‘ahaṁ saṅkhārā, mama saṅkhārā’ti pariyuṭṭhaṭṭhāyino, te saṅkhārā vipariṇamanti aññathā honti.
root_text.sn22.1:13.1: Viññāṇaṁ attato samanupassati, viññāṇavantaṁ vā attānaṁ; attani vā viññāṇaṁ, viññāṇasmiṁ vā attānaṁ.
root_text.sn22.1:13.3: Tassa ‘ahaṁ viññāṇaṁ, mama viññāṇan’ti pariyuṭṭhaṭṭhāyino, taṁ viññāṇaṁ vipariṇamati aññathā hoti.
root_text.sn22.1:13.5: Evaṁ kho, gahapati, āturakāyo ceva hoti āturacitto ca.
root_text.sn22.1:14.1: Kathañca, gahapati, āturakāyo hi kho hoti no ca āturacitto?
root_text.sn22.1:14.2: Idha, gahapati, sutavā ariyasāvako ariyānaṁ dassāvī ariyadhammassa kovido ariyadhamme suvinīto sappurisānaṁ dassāvī sappurisadhammassa kovido sappurisadhamme suvinīto
root_text.sn22.1:14.3: na rūpaṁ attato samanupassati, na rūpavantaṁ vā attānaṁ; na attani vā rūpaṁ, na rūpasmiṁ vā attānaṁ.
root_text.sn22.1:14.4: ‘Ahaṁ rūpaṁ, mama rūpan’ti na pariyuṭṭhaṭṭhāyī hoti.
root_text.sn22.1:14.5: Tassa ‘ahaṁ rūpaṁ, mama rūpan’ti apariyuṭṭhaṭṭhāyino, taṁ rūpaṁ vipariṇamati aññathā hoti.
root_text.sn22.1:15.1: Na vedanaṁ attato samanupassati, na vedanāvantaṁ vā attānaṁ; na attani vā vedanaṁ, na vedanāya vā attānaṁ.
root_text.sn22.1:15.2: ‘Ahaṁ vedanā, mama vedanā’ti na pariyuṭṭhaṭṭhāyī hoti.
root_text.sn22.1:15.3: Tassa ‘ahaṁ vedanā, mama vedanā’ti apariyuṭṭhaṭṭhāyino, sā vedanā vipariṇamati aññathā hoti.
root_text.sn22.1:16.1: Na saññaṁ attato samanupassati, na saññāvantaṁ vā attānaṁ; na attani vā saññaṁ, na saññāya vā attānaṁ.
root_text.sn22.1:16.2: ‘Ahaṁ saññā, mama saññā’ti na pariyuṭṭhaṭṭhāyī hoti.
root_text.sn22.1:16.3: Tassa ‘ahaṁ saññā, mama saññā’ti apariyuṭṭhaṭṭhāyino, sā saññā vipariṇamati aññathā hoti.
root_text.sn22.1:17.1: Na saṅkhāre attato samanupassati, na saṅkhāravantaṁ vā attānaṁ; na attani vā saṅkhāre, na saṅkhāresu vā attānaṁ.
root_text.sn22.1:17.2: ‘Ahaṁ saṅkhārā, mama saṅkhārā’ti na pariyuṭṭhaṭṭhāyī hoti.
root_text.sn22.1:17.3: Tassa ‘ahaṁ saṅkhārā, mama saṅkhārā’ti apariyuṭṭhaṭṭhāyino, te saṅkhārā vipariṇamanti aññathā honti.
root_text.sn22.1:18.1: Na viññāṇaṁ attato samanupassati, na viññāṇavantaṁ vā attānaṁ; na attani vā viññāṇaṁ, na viññāṇasmiṁ vā attānaṁ.
root_text.sn22.1:18.2: ‘Ahaṁ viññāṇaṁ, mama viññāṇan’ti na pariyuṭṭhaṭṭhāyī hoti.
root_text.sn22.1:18.3: Tassa ‘ahaṁ viññāṇaṁ, mama viññāṇan’ti apariyuṭṭhaṭṭhāyino, taṁ viññāṇaṁ vipariṇamati aññathā hoti.
root_text.sn22.1:18.5: Evaṁ kho, gahapati, āturakāyo hoti no ca āturacitto”ti.
root_text.sn22.1:19.2: Attamano nakulapitā gahapati āyasmato sāriputtassa bhāsitaṁ abhinandīti.

translation_text.sn22.1:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Bhaggas at Crocodile’s Bellow, in the deer park at Bhesakaḷā’s Wood.
translation_text.sn22.1:1.3: Then the householder Nakula’s father went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to the Buddha:
translation_text.sn22.1:2.1: “Sir, I’m an old man, elderly and senior. I’m advanced in years and have reached the final stage of life. My body is ailing and I’m constantly unwell.
translation_text.sn22.1:2.2: I hardly ever get to see the esteemed mendicants.
translation_text.sn22.1:2.3: May the Buddha please advise me
translation_text.sn22.1:2.5: It will be for my lasting welfare and happiness.”
translation_text.sn22.1:3.1: “That’s so true, householder! That’s so true, householder!
translation_text.sn22.1:3.2: For this body is ailing, swaddled in its shell.
translation_text.sn22.1:3.3: If anyone dragging around this body claimed to be healthy even for an hour, what is that but foolishness?
translation_text.sn22.1:3.4: So you should train like this:
translation_text.sn22.1:3.5: ‘Though my body is ailing, my mind will be healthy.’
translation_text.sn22.1:4.1: And then the householder Nakula’s father approved and agreed with what the Buddha said. He got up from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right. Then he went up to Venerable Sāriputta, bowed, and sat down to one side. Sāriputta said to him:
translation_text.sn22.1:4.2: “Householder, your faculties are so very clear,
translation_text.sn22.1:4.3: and your complexion is pure and bright.
translation_text.sn22.1:4.4: Did you get to hear a Dhamma talk in the Buddha’s presence today?”
translation_text.sn22.1:5.2: Just now the Buddha anointed me with the deathless ambrosia of a Dhamma talk.”
translation_text.sn22.1:5.3: “But with what kind of ambrosial Dhamma talk did the Buddha anoint you?”
translation_text.sn22.1:5.4: So Nakula’s father told Sāriputta all that had happened, and said,
translation_text.sn22.1:6.8: “That’s the ambrosial Dhamma talk with which I was anointed by the Buddha.”
translation_text.sn22.1:7.1: “But didn’t you feel the need to ask the Buddha the further question:
translation_text.sn22.1:7.2: ‘Sir, how do you define someone ailing in body and ailing in mind, and someone ailing in body and healthy in mind’?”
translation_text.sn22.1:7.3: “Sir, we would travel a long way to learn the meaning of this statement in the presence of Venerable Sāriputta.
translation_text.sn22.1:7.4: May Venerable Sāriputta himself please clarify the meaning of this.”
translation_text.sn22.1:8.1: “Well then, householder, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.”
translation_text.sn22.1:9.1: “And how is one ailing in body and ailing in mind?
translation_text.sn22.1:9.2: It’s when an unlearned ordinary person has not seen the noble ones, and is neither skilled nor trained in the qualities of a noble one. They’ve not seen true persons, and are neither skilled nor trained in the qualities of a true person.
translation_text.sn22.1:9.3: They regard form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form.
translation_text.sn22.1:9.4: They’re obsessed with the thought: ‘I am form, form is mine!’
translation_text.sn22.1:9.5: But that form of theirs decays and perishes,
translation_text.sn22.1:9.6: which gives rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress.
translation_text.sn22.1:10.1: They regard feeling as self, self as having feeling, feeling in self, or self in feeling.
translation_text.sn22.1:10.2: They’re obsessed with the thought: ‘I am feeling, feeling is mine!’
translation_text.sn22.1:10.3: But that feeling of theirs decays and perishes,
translation_text.sn22.1:10.4: which gives rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress.
translation_text.sn22.1:11.1: They regard perception as self, self as having perception, perception in self, or self in perception.
translation_text.sn22.1:11.2: They’re obsessed with the thought: ‘I am perception, perception is mine!’
translation_text.sn22.1:11.3: But that perception of theirs decays and perishes,
translation_text.sn22.1:11.4: which gives rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress.
translation_text.sn22.1:12.1: They regard choices as self, self as having choices, choices in self, or self in choices.
translation_text.sn22.1:12.2: They’re obsessed with the thought: ‘I am choices, choices are mine!’
translation_text.sn22.1:12.3: But those choices of theirs decay and perish,
translation_text.sn22.1:12.4: which gives rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress.
translation_text.sn22.1:13.1: They regard consciousness as self, self as having consciousness, consciousness in self, or self in consciousness.
translation_text.sn22.1:13.2: They’re obsessed with the thought: ‘I am consciousness, consciousness is mine!’
translation_text.sn22.1:13.3: But that consciousness of theirs decays and perishes,
translation_text.sn22.1:13.4: which gives rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress.
translation_text.sn22.1:13.5: That’s how one is ailing in body and ailing in mind.
translation_text.sn22.1:14.1: And how is one ailing in body and healthy in mind?
translation_text.sn22.1:14.2: It’s when a learned noble disciple has seen the noble ones, and is skilled and trained in the teaching of the noble ones. They’ve seen true persons, and are skilled and trained in the teaching of the true persons.
translation_text.sn22.1:14.3: They don’t regard form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form.
translation_text.sn22.1:14.4: They’re not obsessed with the thought: ‘I am form, form is mine!’
translation_text.sn22.1:14.5: So when that form of theirs decays and perishes,
translation_text.sn22.1:14.6: it doesn’t give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress.
translation_text.sn22.1:15.1: They don’t regard feeling as self, self as having feeling, feeling in self, or self in feeling.
translation_text.sn22.1:15.2: They’re not obsessed with the thought: ‘I am feeling, feeling is mine!’
translation_text.sn22.1:15.3: So when that feeling of theirs decays and perishes,
translation_text.sn22.1:15.4: it doesn’t give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress.
translation_text.sn22.1:16.1: They don’t regard perception as self, self as having perception, perception in self, or self in perception.
translation_text.sn22.1:16.2: They’re not obsessed with the thought: ‘I am perception, perception is mine!’
translation_text.sn22.1:16.3: So when that perception of theirs decays and perishes,
translation_text.sn22.1:16.4: it doesn’t give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress.
translation_text.sn22.1:17.1: They don’t regard choices as self, self as having choices, choices in self, or self in choices.
translation_text.sn22.1:17.2: They’re not obsessed with the thought: ‘I am choices, choices are mine!’
translation_text.sn22.1:17.3: So when those choices of theirs decay and perish,
translation_text.sn22.1:17.4: it doesn’t give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress.
translation_text.sn22.1:18.1: They don’t regard consciousness as self, self as having consciousness, consciousness in self, or self in consciousness.
translation_text.sn22.1:18.2: They’re not obsessed with the thought: ‘I am consciousness, consciousness is mine!’
translation_text.sn22.1:18.3: So when that consciousness of theirs decays and perishes,
translation_text.sn22.1:18.4: it doesn’t give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress.
translation_text.sn22.1:18.5: That’s how an individual is ailing in body and healthy in mind.”
translation_text.sn22.1:19.2: Satisfied, Nakula’s father approved what Sāriputta said.

reference_text.sn22.1:1.1: bj15.2, cck17.1, csp1ed13.1, csp2ed13.1, dr17.1, ms13S3_2, msdiv1, ndp13.241, pts-vp-pli3.1, sya17.1, vri25.1
